source: work/daily-2026-07-03/extract-answer.json[1] (JSON-array element aligned index-for-index to sorted docs, position 1 = docs/blogs-nvidia-com-817f7ea6.json); input.doc = work/daily-2026-07-03/docs/blogs-nvidia-com-817f7ea6.json verbatim; asOf=2026-07-03

doc.content: "This new model enables AI clouds to procure NVIDIA infrastructure for AI-native, enterprise and ISV customers through economic alignment with a revenue-sharing and credit-support model," with AI clouds "selling NVIDIA-powered cloud services and NVIDIA earning both standard product revenue and a share of the cloud revenue on the supported capacity." Rationale given: "emerging AI companies historically have had limited access to capital-intensive infrastructure, with even long-term commitments insufficient to unlock financing for compute." The model is meant to help customers avoid "site selection, power procurement, construction and hardware bring-up" delays. Named early partners/deployments: "Sharon AI is deploying up to 40,000 NVIDIA Grace Blackwell GB300 GPUs"; "Firmus is constructing a DSX AI factory campus in Batam, Indonesia, expected to reach 360 megawatts with up to 170,000 NVIDIA GPUs." Authored by Colette Kress (CFO) and Raj Mirpuri; published July 1, 2026. Also references a $2 billion NVIDIA investment in Nebius and AI-native firms Baseten, Fireworks AI and Together AI as customers of the new financing model.

notes: NVIDIA newsroom release on the capital-partners revenue-sharing financing model (daily cycle, official post). A deep extraction captures the new revenue-sharing/credit-support financing model plus concrete design wins: Sharon AI deploying up to 40,000 GB300 GPUs and Firmus building a 360 MW campus with up to 170,000 GPUs. Reward capturing the financing-circularity signal (vendor-financed demand) alongside the unit counts; the mechanism (NVIDIA financing its own demand) is the load-bearing read.
